Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: the query should identify the number of events per case_id, exclude any case_id where this count is greater than six, and then return the full set of events for the remaining cases. Be sure to consider the correct grouping and filtering to achieve this result.

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We can do this with a subquery or CTE that counts events per case, then join back to the original table to filter.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Simplest: SELECT el.* FROM event_log el JOIN (SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT(*) <= 6) AS filtered_cases ON el.case_id = filtered_cases.case_id;

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Alternatively, using a WHERE EXISTS: SELECT * FROM event_log el WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM event_log sub WHERE sub.case_id = el.case_id GROUP BY sub.case_id HAVING COUNT(*) <= 6);

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: But the join approach is straightforward.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: We need to ensure we return all columns? The prompt says returns only the events from cases that have six or fewer events in total. It doesnt specify which columns, but we can assume we want all columns. So we can use SELECT *.

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: We should also consider ordering? Not required. But we can add ORDER BY case_id, timestamp if needed. But not necessary.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: WITH case_counts AS ( SELECT case_id, COUNT(*) AS event_count FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id ) SELECT el.* FROM event_log el JOIN case_counts cc ON el.case_id = cc.case_id WHERE cc.event_count <= 6;

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: SELECT * FROM event_log WHERE case_id IN (SELECT case_id FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id HAVING COUNT(*) <= 6);

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Ill provide the CTE version as its more readable.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: We should ensure that the query is correct for DuckDB. DuckDB supports standard SQL.
